sentences.text: Cattle cars to Czechoslovakia.
However, I wanted to mention one thing.
That we, the 300 women, arrived in Auschwitz with our names.
And after the few weeks in Auschwitz, in spite of everything, we left Auschwitz with our names.
We never became a number in Auschwitz, like all other people who arrived in Auschwitz.
They were immediately tattooed with their number.
Nobody was-- nobody had a name, but everybody became like a number.
We left Auschwitz with our names.
I don't remember exactly.
But it was a long journey, maybe three days, whatever.
It seemed endless.
Over there was very bad because it was also very cold.
In the wagons, we felt cold.
But after we arrived in Brunnlitz, little did we know who Brunnlitz-- where Brunnlitz.
There, again, when they-- when we left the cattle cars, they marched us-- I mean, they, the Nazi guards-- to the factory.
When we entered the factory, there were, again, a few Nazi guards and SS women.
And among them, there was, towering, was Oskar Schindler.
He looked at us, maybe, with disbelief because we did not look like human.
But he quickly looked directly at us.
And I would quote, he said, you don't have to worry any more.
You are with me again.
And this was the first time where our hope, where our faith again entered our bodies.
Because seeing Oskar Schindler, remembering somebody who cared, somebody who had compassion.
I will only mention another thing because all the people like to dwell what Oskar Schindler did.
And not such nice things do they mention about Oskar Schindler.
The way I know, from my experience, who was Oskar Schindler?
Oskar Schindler was a very young man.
He was a very good-looking man.
He was born in Czechoslovakia.
When-- in '38 when the Germans occupied Czechoslovakia, he joined the Nazi party, no question about it.
He became very active in the-- in the party.
But when Germany occupied Poland, he certainly came to Poland with one purpose.
He was an opportunist.
He came to Poland to make a fortune.
Oskar Schindler was a man who loved money, who loved women, who loved to drink, who loved to smoke.
He always cared to be the center of attraction.
His charm-- with his charm-- and to top everything off, he was a master in bribery.
And with his charm and knowing how to bribe, he achieved the impossible, the unbelievable thing.
Number two, very few people know about it, how he grew up, where he grew up.
Because I will tell you later on, a neighbor of my sister in Israel heard from this neighbor the details because this gentleman grew up with Schindler in Czechoslovakia.
Oskar Schindler and his sister Adeline, they grew up in a neighborhood.
They played.
They went to school with two other little boys their age.
And the father of the two boys was a rabbi.
Schindler was always a restless boy.
When he was in trouble with his father, he would run to the rabbi for help.
So he actually grew up knowing that Jews have no horns, that Jews are human beings.
And this, I believe, did reflect-- in spite that he came to make money, in spite-- because using people for forced labor, it had a purpose.
By doing this, he was saving our lives.
He was-- as I say, he loved to be the center of attraction.
We must remember and never forget.
And again, I'm going to say, from our experience, we never saw him raise a hand or raise his voice in camp.
He was using compassion.
Not showing it, but there it was.
So Oskar Schindler did unbelievable things.
He amassed a lot of money.
But the end, we know also, what did he do with all the money?
He spent all the money saving people.
So we cannot say that he was just an SS-man.
Oskar Schindler was remarkable man.
He did remarkable things.
And towards the end, he was more and more risking his life.
Yet, he was determined, as much as possible, to save those people.
And those who were in Brunnlitz can tell you a lot of things-- how he did it, what he did it-- Can you tell me?
I believe, number one, that we know the young boy who was the master, making the stamp.
When he came up-- one thing we must also remember, that Schindler-- whatever he did-- from day number one, he did surround himself with many Jewish people.
Business people, engineers, the most talented people, they became his confidants.
And whatever he did achieve, it was done with the advice of our people.
But of course, our people couldn't do nothing.
Schindler did it.
And he was a human being.
In spite of everything, being and drinking with the murderers, he remained a human being, a caring person.
And this we cannot forget.
In Brunnlitz, again, although my husband did mention that we could not produce because over there was only the munitions factory.
I was assigned immediately to the biggest punch-press, the most complicated machine.
In [NON-ENGLISH], I was working by pots and pans.
And over day, I became the number one expert to operate the punch-press.
Yes, we used to see Schindler.
But we used to see him less.
The only thing, they were still till the last moment-- and we must remember, this is already '45.
Of course, the Nazis knew how the war was coming to the end.
But the Nazi women, till the last moment, what they did, how they were treating us-- because they were certain, you know, that the day would come.
As you already know about it, the mass grave was prepared for us.
Nobody didn't enter to the Nazi's mind that maybe something can happen unexpected, and some people will survive them over there.
They were positive.
They were sure nobody will come out from there alive.
This is not even nice.
Like for instance, we were working.
And after all, they had nothing to do.
So they found things to do.
When they would-- when they decided, they would come and they would say, this row of machines, whoever's operating now, you are going to the latrine.
Not when you need it, but when they felt.
And also, the way they did it-- so dehumanized, so to degrade us.
It is unbelievable.
So they would-- we would follow her because the latrine was in the courtyard outside the main building.
So she would say, now-- is only [GERMAN] kommander.
Now, this sounds horrible.
But that's how they looked at us.
They treated us less than human.
Then, they would take us, another group, another time.
And I was [GERMAN] kommander.
This is awful!
That's what they were doing.
And when we were waiting in, in line, because we were entering one at a time-- and this was in the spring.
And we saw the sun.
And we turned our eyes to the skies.
And she would take the whip.
And she would say, the sun does not shine for you.
Little did we know what she meant.
Because she was sure, you know, any day they order will come and we will be wiped out.
So there were many things, which it is embarrassing to talk what they were doing to us.

And when we were working over there, when the day came before the liberation, it was for us very sad that evening when Schindler spoke to us, saying goodbye to us, warning us about certain things, giving advice.
And he even expressed he must leave.
And by the way, the last few months, Mrs.
Schindler was with him.
And she was very much helpful.
She was doing a lot of work-- unbelievable work.
How she was nursing those people from those frozen wagons.
And she did it with her own hands.
But also, you must remember that time was doing its own marks.
We were getting weaker.
We were getting-- many people were getting sick after this many years without any medical help or whatever.
So the-- we felt the ending of our lives.
If the war would have lasted longer, I don't think so anybody could have survived this.
It was the turn of it, that the war was coming to an end, that Schindler managed-- managed to get rid of the Commandant Leopold by falsifying the papers.
And this took a masterminding to come up with an idea how to get rid of such a murderer like Leopold.
Schindler managed with his people.
And it is amazing.
This young man who falsified the stamp for this document for-- for this Leopold, this young man, after the war, you know, he and his brother immigrated, at that time, Palestine, then Israel.
You know, only a few short years, this young man retired from his position as a Supreme Court judge in Israel.
So we had many talented people.
Who made the stamp.
This--
Moishe-- what was his last name?
I knew him so well-- Moishe.
Kuba would remember  it.
Yes.
Belski.
Belski.
Moishe Belski.
B-E-L-- B-E-L-S-K-E.
Some people with Y.
But it was with a I.
Not a E, with a I in Poland.
When Schindler was leaving, because we were, again, alone.
And we did not know what to do next.
To deal with freedom is not easy.
Especially, we were so dehumanized.
We did not know how to cope with life.
And the worst thing, we were scared.
Where do you go?
You don't have a country.
You don't have anybody who is waiting.
So what do you do?
So we were very much-- instead to celebrate, we were afraid what we going to do.
However, again, thanks God to our people, there was always somebody who would give you support with courage to go on and so on.
And this was a big help.
Because if you-- you cannot imagine, in the darkest moment where we were almost ready to give up, our people-- the spirit of-- the Jewish spirit is unmeasurable.
How people would come up with a joke, with a song.
Because after we were counted, most of the time, we would return to our barrack-- there was always somebody missing.
And this was a very difficult time, how to accept reality.
Yet, out of nowhere, somebody would come up, tell a joke, tell a, a song to break the stillness.
And this helped us too because of our people.
The Jewish people are very unique.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, I will tell you.
The only thing I cannot talk on this subject much because we-- my sister and I-- a friend of ours and her sister-- and a group of men-- 14 men, four girls.
Because among the men I did not know, in Schindler's factory was a man who was a brother of my brother-in-law.
When we were-- he used to see us-- because the women were kept so separated, we could not-- when I was even working at the machine, there was always the woman.
And I had three boys, men, who had to help out, carry those big shells.
I understand those were parts to the rocket V2.
It was a very sophisticated work.
And I was operating the machine, which the machine was going constantly.
And my hands were going like that.
A split of a second, I could have made, you know, the whole machine break.
Because if it didn't go directly-- and this was a very complicated thing.
So we were over there-- I mentioned something else.
I said, was there-- did leadership develop?
Leadership-- so when this brother of my brother, the young man, he says, he decided with some other men he made up.
And they are leaving.
They're going to Krakow the very first few days after the liberation.
And he said, I would wanted you to join us.
Because they were-- not only I-- other people were certain.
I had brothers.
And what they going to tell my family?
They left us in a camp somewheres?
So he tried to convince us.
And we did.
How did you get to Krakow?
By foot.
And when we arrived in Poland already, number one, we encountered all kinds of unpleasant things during the journey home, even the confrontation with the Russian soldiers.
It's very embittered to talk about it.
But it was meant.
We survived.
We came to Krakow.
And when I went to our former neighbor, and she greeted us with such hostility.
Are you still alive?
Like, they would be afraid that I might come to their home and ask for some possessions what my parents left.
The only thing in Krakow-- thank God, Krakow was liberated in January.
And the Jewish people over there, I saw for the first time leadership, where they established a center.
And they were helping out the people who were coming from the camps because we had nowhere to find a place to have a roof over our head.
So--
I was in Krakow from '45, end of May, beginning of June, when we arrived.
And I left Poland early in '46.
I went-- this was the time where I met my husband, after the liberation in Krakow.
And we decided to leave Krakow after the pogrom and other things.
There was no future for us.
And we went with a small group through the Briha.
We left Poland illegally to Czechoslovakia, from Czechoslovakia to Germany because our aim was to reach Bamberg, Germany.
And we succeeded, yes.
In Bamberg, when we arrived in '46 until we left Germany in 1949.
Life in Bamberg-- you know, we were in the DP camps.

And this was a little bit unbelievable too because we, the, the survivors, after years of surviving those horrible death camps, we still came out from those death camps with hope, determination to start a new life.
We even expected that the world will embrace us with compassion.
And yet, we were confronted by the whole world, after the liberation, with this belief-- we were even accused of exaggeration, that something like that could not happen.
And this, after a while, we could understand.
Because who would or could understand?
Something like that happen in the middle of civilization?
In the 20th century?
So we saw the world had no compassion, no sympathy.
We relied on our own.
And only through hard, very hard work and determination, we built a new life.
And when we arrived in this-- by the way, our first child was born in Germany.
And this was a miracle.
Few people are aware or they do realize that the problem for us, the survivors, never ended.
Most of the women who survived the camps could not bear children because for whatever the Nazis did, either with those chemicals in our food or whatever.
I was very fortunate.
And that's why I said, we were very fortunate that we decided and we started to build a new life and start a new family.
Many, many women needed medical attention and could bear children.
However, a lot could never return to a normal life.
Many survivors who were liberated, for them, it was too late.
They could-- there were those who could not cope with life.
And they had to be institutionalized.
There were many problems what we faced right after the liberation.
The only thing what I can say, and to me, it's very important.
Because after the liberation-- the children born to us after the liberation, they became the justification to continue life.
Their existence, their achievements, was our triumph.
And we only hope and pray that they will continue the Jewish tradition from generation to generation with pride, dignity befitting our people.
We already, a little bit, are stepping aside.
We are permitting the second generation to carry on the tradition, to carry on to remember the Holocaust.
Because the Holocaust was the most shameful tragedy since men walked on Earth.
We have no excuse.
And it doesn't matter.
Not only-- this does not only rest on our shoulders, but all Jews have the responsibility to carry on, never to forget what happened.
Because they are millions, hundreds, thousands, hundreds of thousands of names that nobody was left.
There is nobody even to say a prayer after them.
So we, my husband and I, when we settled in the United States, in the beginning it was very difficult.
You must remember how difficult.
We experienced such unbelievable horrors, such deprivations, that it left permanent scars on our bodies and on our souls.
And yet, the world did not want to hear us, nobody.
Again, we relied on ourselves.
And we tried to carry on.
It became like our life obligation to share our experience with many people.
Mainly, we are trying to reach the young people because today, they are students.
They are young.
We have to remember, tomorrow, they are our future leaders.
And by sharing our opinion, by knowing what happened, and more you know, more you want to learn about it.
By knowing you would be and will be able to prevent from happening again because since it happened, a tragedy like that must not happen again.
About [?
Mila? ?] Oh.
Do I have a couple minutes?
Our experience is-- unending.
I want you to know, when you returned to Poland, I don't know if I mentioned.
I am from a large family.
We are four surviving sisters.
My three sisters live in Israel.
When we returned from Brunnlitz to Krakow-- and as I said, there were already people liberated a short time before.
And somehow, we found out from somebody that one of my sisters and my brother-in-law did survive the war in hiding by a Polish farmer.
They were 3 and 1/2 years completely in hiding.
They didn't see the world outside.
But the Polish farmer is unbelievable.
He risked the life-- not only his life, but the life of his family.
You must know that in Poland, somehow they did-- the Polish people suspect that he is hiding.
So he was tortured and he was ordered to tell whom he's hiding and where the Jews are he is hiding.
He never give out their names.
And that's why my sister, my brother-in-law was saved by such a dedicated Polish farmer.
When we found out that my sister survived, my sister-- the one who survived with me all the time.
You remember, I mentioned we were taken from home?
Yes.
We were always in the camps.
And when we decided to go to the farm to find the sister and my brother-in-law, so we went to the railroad station.
We bought tickets and waiting, just waiting for the train.
However, a miracle happened at that time.
While we were waiting for the train, we notice under the bench in the railroad station there was something black, something moving.
But it was moving.
Curiosity brought us closer to look what's under the bench.
What's moving?
This moving was my sister, who returned from different concentration camps.
And she arrived in the open trains.
From the smoke and everything, she was black.
When she arrived in Krakow, she was so exhausted, so hungry, so she decided the first thing to do to go and take a rest.
And she crawled under the bench.
We never went to the farm that day to look for the other sister because we brought her to our room.
She was so tired, so hungry.
We fed her.
We washed her.
And she went to sleep.
For two days, she didn't want to leave the bed.
But thank God, you know?
In 1945, I had a cousin who was very active in the Zionist organization.
He came to Krakow.
And I was left in Krakow with the older sister and myself.
And that time, I met Kuba.
But this cousin took those two sisters, illegally, to Germany.
So they wound up in the English zone in Germany.
And the youngest sister, there was only one legal boat what was permitted by the English forces that they went.
She was on the legal boat.
And she arrived on Youth Aliyah in Palestine.
The other sister, she was trying.
She was on a couple of boats and was returned back because the boat was caught by the English army.
And they were returned back.
Then, she went to Munich.
And she studied nursing.
And after she finished nursing school, she became a nurse.
This was the time when I gave birth to our first child.
And then, a couple weeks later, after I gave birth to our first child, she went.
Again, she tried.
And she managed to get to Israel.
